questions.description: "The doctrine of ostensible authority, the Rule in Turquand's case and, to a lesser extent, Regulation 6 of the…
questions.text: "The doctrine of ostensible authority, the Rule in Turquand's case and, to a lesser extent, Regulation 6 of the European Communities (Companies) Regulations 1973 (SI 163 of 1973) provide a satisfactory solution to the practical problems caused by the fact that although companies are separate legal persons, they can only act through human beings." Discuss this statement. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Sean has for the last 5 years operated as a sole trader as a wood turner,… Company.
questions.text: Sean has for the last 5 years operated as a sole trader as a wood turner, making wooden bowls and assorted other goods from wood. He employs six people and his business has been very successful. Sean comes to you for advice and during the course of the consultation he tells you a number of things about his business: - Sean is considering incorporating the business but is not sure of the advantages and disadvantages of doing this. - He is about to acquire a new factory outlet, whereby the products will be made in the same location as they are sold and members of the public can come and see him and his employees at work. Sean will need to borrow from his bank to acquire the new premises. - Recently, one of his employees, Bill, received a nasty cut from a chisel which severed an artery and caused him to be off work for two months, and although Sean's insurance company paid out on the claim, Sean had a few sleepless nights worrying over the claim until he received their letter confirming it was covered in full by the policy. - Sean's longest serving employee, Fred, has for some time been pressing Sean to form a partnership with him so he can share in the success of the business. Although Sean is agreeable to giving Fred a slice of the action, he is very uncomfortable with forming a partnership with Fred. - Whereas Sean has a book-keeper he has never bothered having his accounts audited. You are required to advise Sean on the [advantages and disadvantages]{.underline} of incorporating his business as a private company limited by shares, having particular regard to the matters raised by Sean during the consultation. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: David, Deborah and Cindy are equal shareholders, holding 50 shares each in… Regulation, Company.
questions.text: David, Deborah and Cindy are equal shareholders, holding 50 shares each in Foxtrot Tango Limited (the "Company"), established by their late mother Madge, and acquired by them under her will. The Company is in the business of retailing carpet and its only two directors are David and Deborah who also work in the Company. Cindy left the country a number of years ago and lives with her husband in San Francisco and over the years has not had any involvement in the Company. Once a year she contacts her brother and sister to ask how the Company is doing and usually, following that call, David and Deborah declare a dividend which the Company pays to Cindy, averaging over €50,000 per annum for the last three years. Last Christmas when Cindy came home to Ireland she tentatively enquired from David and Deborah whether they would be interested in buying her shares in the Company. They said they would have to think about it because it was hard to know what the shares were worth, given the economic downturn. David and Deborah come to you for advice when Cindy contacted them again and announced that she had sold her shares in the Company to Joe, a local businessman in the same town where the Company has its premises. A couple of days later David and Deborah were approached by Joe who gave them a stock transfer form signed by Cindy transferring her 50 shares to him, and he asked them to register him as a member. David and Deborah are unhappy to share the family business with an outsider and ask you for your advice. On examination of the Company's articles of association, you see that Regulation 3 of Part II of Table A applies: "The directors may, in their absolute discretion, and without assigning any reason therefore, decline to register any transfer of any share, whether or not it is a fully paid share". You are required to advise David and Deborah on their rights and obligations in relation to the transfer of shares to Joe. In your answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: "While the traditional position has been that company directors owe their duties to their company and to the company…
questions.text: "While the traditional position has been that company directors owe their duties to their company and to the company alone, increasingly it is being recognised that directors can in particular circumstances owe duties to other persons too." Discuss this statement. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: "When a liquidator brings an application under section 150 of the Companies Act 1990 to have a de jure director, a de…
questions.text: "When a liquidator brings an application under section 150 of the Companies Act 1990 to have a de jure director, a de facto director or a shadow director of an insolvent company restricted, the making of an order will be inevitable unless the person can satisfy the High Court that he or she has acted honestly and responsibly." You are required to identify and discuss the key legal issues raised by this statement. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Bob, Patrick, and Patty are directors of B-P-P Limited (the "Company")… Company, Shareholder.
questions.text: Bob, Patrick, and Patty are directors of B-P-P Limited (the "Company") which runs an estate agency. They are also shareholders in the Company and each hold 25 of the 100 issued shares, the other 25 shares being held by John. John is a solicitor and has never been a director of the company, being content to be just a shareholder. The premises from which the Company operates is owned by John and leased to the Company at a very favourable rent for the Company. Bob, Patrick and Patty have traditionally recommended that purchasers and vendors of properties that go through the agency use John's solicitor's practice for their conveyancing and this has been a very lucrative source of revenue for John. John would in turn recommend to his clients that they use the Company as their estate agent. In recent times, however, John's recommendations have been few and far between and Bob, Patrick and Patty have been recommending other firms of solicitors to clients of the Company as it seems to them that these other solicitors are in a better position than John to make cross-referrals back to the Company. On average, the Company would have made about 30 referrals to John every month. For the last 6 months, however, this has fallen steadily and for the last month there were only 5 referrals to him. John was unsure as to why referrals were falling off and had blamed it on the economic downturn until one of his friends in another firm of solicitors (who did not know John was a shareholder in the Company) mentioned that he and the Company were cross-referring clients very successfully. John was very annoyed and confronted Bob, Patrick and Patty, claiming that in ceasing to maintain his level of referrals they were acting in disregard of his interests as a shareholder, especially having regard to the below market rent he charged the Company for its premises. John also objected to not being told about their decision to make referrals elsewhere. Their response was that they were acting in the interests of the Company and had never asked for a lower rent, and that he was welcome to seek to review the rent if he thought it was not at market value. They also indicated that if this was John's attitude, they would not make any referrals to him - a statement which provoked John to claim that they were acting oppressively. You are required to advise on the following matters: (a) whether John has a remedy under section 205 of the Companies Act 1963; [and]{.underline} (b) whether your answer would be different if the articles of association contained a provision that the Company would support shareholders' businesses by referring clients to them; [and]{.underline} (c) whether your answer would be different if a shareholders' agreement contained a provision that the Company would support shareholders' businesses by referring clients to them. Your answer must address each of the three questions at (a), (b) and (c) and must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Richard entered into a contract with Construct & Develop Limited (the… Company.
questions.text: Richard entered into a contract with Construct & Develop Limited (the "Company") to level a site that he owns and to construct a multi-story car park on the site. Richard was required to pay the Company €4.5 million to carry out the works, to be paid in three equal instalments of €1.5 million each. Richard was concerned at having to pay such a large sum to the Company and it was agreed that he would be given some comfort in the nature of a charge over a massive crane that the Company owns. Accordingly, it was provided in the agreement that "as security for the performance of the Company 's obligations under this contract, at all times when the Company's crane is on the site, it shall be deemed to be the property " of Richard. Within weeks of paying the Company its first instalment of €1.5 million and without any work having been carried out to the site, the Revenue Commissioners successfully petitioned the High Court to have the Company wound up for failure to pay its debts. It transpires that the Company was hopelessly insolvent and owed the Revenue Commissioners €6 million and despite having paid them the bulk of the €1.5 million, this had not prevented the Revenue Commissioners from petitioning to have the Company wound up. The official liquidator, after informing Richard of his appointment, sought to make an arrangement to have the Company's crane removed from Richard's property. Richard refuses to allow the liquidator onto the site and seeks to claim ownership of the crane, which he proposes to sell to defray the €1.5 million he paid to the Company. The official liquidator's response is that any interest in the crane that Richard might have is a charge that is void for want of registration under section 99 of the Companies Act, 1963 (the "1963 Act"). You are required to advise Richard: (a) whether the agreement with the Company created a charge and if so, whether it is a floating charge or a fixed charge [and]{.underline} (b) if the agreement did create a charge, whether it was registrable under section 99 of the 1963 Act [and]{.underline} (c) the consequences of non-registration of a registrable charge. Your answer must clearly distinguish between (a), (b) and (c) of this question and you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: "Making a winding-up order on the ground that a company is unable to pay its debts is discretionary: not only should a…
questions.text: "Making a winding-up order on the ground that a company is unable to pay its debts is discretionary: not only should a petitioner satisfy the statutory requirements of section 214 of the Companies Act 1963 but so too should a petitioner be able to show that the debt is bona fide owed to them by the company and that the company does not have a valid cross-claim." You are required to identify and discuss the key aspects of this statement. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: "A company's objects clause and the doctrine of ultravires provide necessary protection for the shareholders and…
questions.text: "A company's objects clause and the doctrine of ultravires provide necessary protection for the shareholders and creditors of companies." Discuss this statement. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Leonard and Larry are directors of LL Builders Limited (the "Company") in… Company, Shareholder.
questions.text: Leonard and Larry are directors of LL Builders Limited (the "Company") in which they are equal 50:50 shareholders. The Company's business has been the purchase, development and resale of period houses. The Company has operated very successfully for the last 10 years; Leonard taking responsibility for the purchase and sale of the properties and Larry looking after the development and building works. Over the last five years Leonard had told Larry that he had put together a portfolio of properties for the Company and that he had been purchasing suitable properties as they came to market so as to ensure a steady supply of properties were available for development by Larry. Larry comes to you for advice. He tells you that he has discovered that Leonard had been acquiring properties not only for the Company but for another company owned by Leonard's son, Frederick. Larry suspects that the better properties (acquired more cheaply with greatest potential for a higher resale value) were siphoned off to Frederick's company. Larry tells you that the Company has three properties on its books now which are worth about 25% less than what the Company paid for them a couple of years ago. Larry believes he has identified two other properties which were destined to be acquired by the Company but instead were diverted to Frederick's company. In contrast to the Company's properties, Larry believes that these other properties are now worth at least as much as Frederick paid for them, if not more. Larry also suspects that Leonard has an interest in Frederick's company. You are required to advise Larry of the Company's remedies against Leonard for breach of his duties as director. In your answer you are required to refer to all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: "A share is an intangible accumulation of rights, interests and obligations".
questions.text: "A share is an intangible accumulation of rights, interests and obligations". You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: "Unlike meetings of members, the meetings of directors in a company are not heavily regulated by the Companies Acts".
questions.text: "Unlike meetings of members, the meetings of directors in a company are not heavily regulated by the Companies Acts". You are required to discuss this statement, stating whether you agree or disagree with it and in your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law in support of your views.

questions.description: For the last 12 years, Jackie operated a florist shop from a unit in a… Company, Shareholder.
questions.text: Jackie's business has failed. For the last 12 years, Jackie operated a florist shop from a unit in a shopping centre outside of Dublin. The business was conducted through Smell That Limited (the "Company") of which Jackie was the sole shareholder and one of two directors, the other director being Jackie's former fiance, Ted. Jackie contacted Ted to tell him the company was insolvent and they agreed to hold a directors' meeting with a view to putting the Company into creditors' voluntary winding up. On meeting with Jackie and Ted, you elicit the following information: - The Company now owes €120,000 in back rent and taxes. - The Company has struggled through the recession, as people have had less disposable income to spend on flowers. The final straw has been an increase in rent in relation to the shop unit the Company rents, as it was increased from €5,000 to €7,000 per month. - The Revenue Commissioners have not been paid VAT on sales, or PRSI or PAYE due on Jackie's salary from the Company for three months. - In addition, the Revenue Commissioners have issued an assessment for €20,000 in relation to back tax owed to it which the Company cannot afford to pay. The circumstances in which this has become due were that Jackie had hired a teenage girl to work a few hours on a Saturday morning, paying her cash in hand. When this was discovered by the Revenue Commissioners, they claimed for lost PRSI and PAYE tax on the girl's earnings over a two-year period. - The Company's books and records have been maintained by a local book-keeper to an acceptable standard, however, on the way to delivering the books and records to the book-keeper for the last 12-month period, Jackie's car was stolen and the books and records destroyed after the joy-rider thieves set fire to the car. - Ted, the Company's second director, has not been active in the business of the Company. He agreed to be a director whilst engaged to Jackie because every company needs two directors, but he has not been active in the Company's business, only signing accounts and forms when asked to do so. Although they have since split up, it was amicable and Ted only agreed to stay on until Jackie could find someone else to become a director. You are required to advise both Jackie and Ted on the likelihood that they will be restricted under section 150 of the Companies Act 1990 and in particular on the defences available to them. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: "Section 205 of the Companies Act 1963 provides the shareholders of a company with a more flexible, more available and…
questions.text: "Section 205 of the Companies Act 1963 provides the shareholders of a company with a more flexible, more available and more powerful remedy against directors' wrongdoing than any or all of the exceptions to the Rule in Foss v Harbottle ''. Discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: Classical Collection Limited (the "Company'') created a debenture in… Mortgage, Company.
questions.text: Classical Collection Limited (the "Company'') created a debenture in favour of Big Bank plc to secure a loan of ‚Ç¨5,000,000 made to the Company to purchase and redevelop a building in Galway where it proposed opening a classical music CD shop. The debenture included a first legal mortgage and charge over the building and contained the usual provision allowing Big Bank plc to appoint a receiver. In order to get the loan, Seamus Mahler, its managing director and sole shareholder, was obliged to give a personal guarantee to Big Bank plc. Seamus was happy to do so because his old school friend, Bob, was a local builder who was undertaking the redevelopment and Seamus was very happy that Bob the builder would have fixed his shop to a very high standard. After redeveloping the premises, opening the shop and struggling with dwindling custom, Seamus had to face the reality that there would never be a sufficient number of high spending classical music lovers in Galway city to make such a shop profitable. After defaulting on the Company's loan repayments for two months, Big Bank plc, which was then owed over ‚Ç¨4,500,000, appointed a receiver. Seamus was shocked when he heard through a local auctioneer that the receiver is now proposing to sell the premises, which the Company had acquired for ‚Ç¨5,000,000, for only ‚Ç¨4,500,000 even though substantial works have been carried out to the building. Seamus is now extremely concerned that not only will the Company's property be sold for less than it paid for it, but that Big Bank plc is also likely to call upon his guarantee to the tune of the shortfall, some ‚Ç¨500,000! Bob is also now regretting his re-acquaintance with his old school friend Seamus, as the Company has only paid Bob ‚Ç¨50,000 of the ‚Ç¨500,000 that the redevelopment cost. Bob believes also that if the receiver could obtain more for the premises, he too could be paid for the building works carried out. You are required to advise the receiver, when disposing of the premises, as to the duties (if any) that he owes to: (a) the Company; (b) the guarantor of the loan, Seamus Mahler; and (c) the Company's largest unsecured creditor, Bob the builder. In your answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: You are approached for advice by Luke who has been appointed the… Consideration, Company.
questions.text: You are approached for advice by Luke who has been appointed the liquidator of Celtic Excesses Limited (the "Company'') which operated as an event management company before the Revenue Commissioners petitioned successfully to have it wound up on the grounds that it was unable to pay its debts. Luke seeks your advice in relation to the following specific matters which have come to his attention: (a) the Company's directors, Pat and Ruth, paid-off Pat's brother Timmy who was owed a ‚Ç¨30,000 trade debt by the Company for food his catering company had supplied, about 15 months before the Company was wound up; (b) four months before the Company was wound up, Ruth and Pat caused the Company to transfer its assets to the value of ‚Ç¨300,000 to another company which they own, at nil consideration; (c) the day after the petition to wind up the Company was presented, which was two weeks before the Court made the winding-up order, Pat and Ruth paid their employees all sums that were due them in respect of back and holiday pay and bonuses because they were losing their jobs. You are required to advise Luke on the company law relating to each of the three matters identified at (a), (b) [and]{.underline} (c) above. Your answer must clearly distinguish the three matters and cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: "Although the Courts in Ireland have consistently re-iterated the principle in Salomon v A Salomon & Co Ltd [1897] AC…
questions.text: "Although the Courts in Ireland have consistently re-iterated the principle in Salomon v A Salomon & Co Ltd [1897] AC 22, the Irish courts have also recognised that the veil of incorporation can, in appropriate circumstances, be lifted and the Irish legislature has also provided for cases where the separate legal personality of a company will be disregarded". Discuss this statement. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law having particular regard to the reference therein to the Irish courts and Irish legislature.

questions.description: Paul is a director of Cars4All Limited (the "Company") which operates a… Fiduciary, Company.
questions.text: Paul is a director of Cars4All Limited (the "Company") which operates a car sales business in South County Dublin. Paul is not a sharehoider in the Company which is owned by a family consortium headed-up by Simon although Paul has worked in the Company for 10 years; first as a car salesman before being promoted on a number of-occasions until finally being appointed a director of the Company with responsibility for sales. Simon, the majority shareholder and Managing Director of the Company, approaches you for advice. It has come to his knowledge that Paul has been "double-jobbing" for the last two years selling second-hand cars through a website fronted by his wife, Jackie. Simon heard from a friend that -Paul has been buying cars that were traded in to the Company at a discounted price and selling them on for a significant profit on his website. He also used the Company's workshop to service them before re-sale, again availing of discounts on the time and materials charged. You are required to advise Simon on the law relating to directors' common law and equitable fiduciary duties and on the Company's remedies against Paul and Jackie. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law and apply these to the facts provided above.

questions.description: "Corporate contractual capacity can be classified under the headings of substantive objects, express powers and implied…
questions.text: "Corporate contractual capacity can be classified under the headings of substantive objects, express powers and implied powers; any corporate activity outside a company's objects and powers is ultra vires and void." You are required io discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: Jack and Jill are directors and 50:50 shareholders in a company called J&J… Mortgage, Company.
questions.text: Jack and Jill are directors and 50:50 shareholders in a company called J&J Acquisitions Limited (the "Purchaser Company") which intends to purchase all of the issued share capital in another company called Investment Properties Limited (the "Target Company'). The Target Company owns an apartment building containing 5 apartments which are leased to tenants. The Purchaser Company agrees to buy the shares in the Target Company from Richard (who is the sole owner of all of the shares in the Target Company) for €3 million. Jack and Jill have secured loan facilities with Big Bank pic (the "Bank") under which the Bank will loan the Purchaser Company €2.5 million. The Purchaser Company has €500,000 of its own funds to make up the difference between the loan and the purchase price of the shares. The-Bank issues a loan agreement addressed to the Purchaser Company which will be the borrower. As security for the loan, the Bank requires: - the guarantee of the Target Company, and - a charge from the Target Company over the apartment building. The loan agreement also states that the Bank's solicitor must be satisfied that the loan agreement and the security for the loan (the guarantee and legal mortgage and charge) comply with section 60 of the Companies Act 1963 (the "1963 Act") and section 31 of Companies Act 1990 (the "1990 Act"). You are required to advise Jack and Jill: (a) on how the guarantee and charge might contravene section 60 of the 1963 Act and section 31 of the 1990 Act, [and]{.underline} (b) on how the transaction can be structured so as to be in compliance with those provisions. : You are required to answer (a) [and]{.underline} (b). Both parts carry equal marks. In your answers you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: "The circumstances in which a retention of title clause will be found to constitute a charge which, if not registered…
questions.text: "The circumstances in which a retention of title clause will be found to constitute a charge which, if not registered pursuant to section 99 of the Companies Act 1963, will be void as against a liquidator or creditor of a company, are now well settled." You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: William's World of Colours Limited (the "Company") is a company engaged in… Company, Director.
questions.text: William's World of Colours Limited (the "Company") is a company engaged in the business of manufacturing and distributing paint to the Irish construction industry. For the past number of years, business has been booming. Two of the Company's customers have had lucrative contracts for the painting of new housing estates. The Company has three directors - William, Peggy and Eugene - and is a single-member private limited company, all shares being owned by William. William is the son of the Company's founder and is the managing director. Peggy, his daughter, is the Sales Director and Eugene is her husband. Eugene is a non-executive director who works as a local self-employed solicitor. His involvement with the Company is very limited and he never attends meetings of the directors. In recent times the Company's fortunes have faltered. Both of its major customers have gone into liquidation, owing the Company several-hundred thousand euro. Both customers were always slow payers, but the Company received nothing from them for months although it continued to supply them with paint. William realised things were bad when the Company's bank manager telephoned him to say that the Company had exceeded its overdraft. William persuaded the bank to extend the overdraft to pay wages and give him time to collect some outstanding debts. The Company chugged along for another month but then William realised that the Company was insolvent and without any hope of an immediate improvement. At that point William placed' the Company into creditors' voluntary liquidation. The Company's liquidator discovered that the Company's record keeping was sporadic and that management accounts had not been prepared with the frequency that would have alerted the Company's directors to the fact its two main customers has not been discharging what was owed by them for some time. You are required to advise on the likelihood that each of the three directors will be made the subject of a restriction order pursuant to Section 150 of the Companies Act 1990. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: You are approached by Larry, the official liquidator appointed to Moe's… Company, Director.
questions.text: You are approached by Larry, the official liquidator appointed to Moe's Motel Limited (the "Company") which was put into compulsory liquidation after the Revenue Commissioners successfully petitioned to have it wound up on the grounds that it was unable to pay its debts. The Company's main asset was a building in the centre of the town of Moat, County Westmeath from which it operated a 50 bedroom hotel. Business had been good until a new motorway was constructed on the Dublin to Galway road, by-passing Moat. Thereafter, the Company's business fell-off significantly, resulting in the failure to remit PAYE, PRSI and VAT to the Revenue Commissioners who are now owed €3.75m. Indeed, all contact between the Revenue Commissioners and the Company's directors, Moe and May, ceased and it was this lack of communication which prompted the petition to wind up the Company. Following his appointment, Larry went to the Company's hotel premises, and discovered that it had been stripped of furniture, fixtures and fittings. On further investigation, Larry learned that May 'had opened up a new hotel having formed a new company called May's Motel Limited ("May's Company") which is located on the outskirts of Moat, immediately adjacent to the exit to the motorway and visible from the motorway. Larry understands that the new hotel has been equipped with the furniture, fixtures and fittings of the Company. Moreover, a search of the Companies Registration Office disclosed that the Company created a fixed and floating charge over the hotel and its assets in favour of Moe to secure the sum of €500,000 allegedly due to him for management services rendered to the company over many years. It appears also to Larry that the Company's creditors are quite happy to transact business with May's Company because all sums owed to them by the Company were discharged by it. Larry seeks your advice in relation to the following specific matters: (a) Larry values the furniture, fixtures and fittings of the Company which were moved to the hotel owned by May's Company at €500,000. He wants them back and you are required to advise him as to the relevant law on this point. (b) Larry values the Company's hotel at €1.5m. He cannot, however, sell the hotel because of the charges in favour of Moe. He wants you to advise him'as tc whether these charges can be sei aside and if they can, the basis for setting them aside. (c) Larry has learned that the Company's largest creditor, a supplier of food, was paid the €230,000 owed to it in the weeks before the Revenue Commissioners presented their petition to have the Company wound up. Larry asks you whether this money can be recovered by him and if it can, the basis for its recovery. You are required to answer (a) [and]{.underline} (b) [and]{.underline} (c). In your answers you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law. -

questions.description: A person may become subject to the duties and obligations of a director of an Irish company in either of three ways -…
questions.text: A person may become subject to the duties and obligations of a director of an Irish company in either of three ways - on being formally appointed a director, - on being found by a Court to be a de facto director; or - on being found by a Court to be a shadow director. You are required to: (a) describe how each of the foregoing three circumstances can arise, [and]{.underline} (b) the nature of the duties and obligations that can result for the person in each of the three circumstances. You are required to answer (a) [and]{.underline} (b). In your answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.
